sentences.grammarNote: 안 (an) placed before a verb or adjective negates it: 안 좋아요 (not good), 안 가요 (not going), 안 먹어요 (not eating). This short negation is casual. The long form 좋지 않아요 (it is not good) sounds slightly more formal.

**피곤하다** (pigонhada) = 'to be tired / fatigued' — a 하다 adjective. Polite present: 피곤해요.
Related words: **졸리다** (jollida) = to be sleepy, **지치다** (jichida) = to be worn out/exhausted. 피곤하다 is the most general and common.
**너무** (neomu) is used here as an intensifier 'so/very' in everyday speech, though its literal meaning is 'too much.' Standing alone as a short exclamation, 너무 피곤해요 is very natural.

때문에 (ttaemune) = 'because of / due to', attached to nouns: 일 때문에 (because of work), 날씨 때문에 (because of the weather). For verbs, use 〜기 때문에: 피곤하기 때문에 (because I'm tired). This causal connector is essential in Korean.

**아까** (akka) = 'a little while ago / earlier' — a time adverb referring to the recent past (within the same day).
**넘어지다** (neomeojida) = 'to fall down / trip and fall' — the -지다 suffix on 넘다 (to cross over/topple) creates a change-of-state verb. Past tense: 넘어졌어요.
Contrast: 떨어지다 (fall from a height), 넘어지다 (stumble/fall while walking). Both are common in everyday contexts.

**다치다** (dachida) = 'to get hurt / injured' — a common verb for physical injury. Past tense: 다쳤어요. It is intransitive: 다리를 다쳤어요 (I hurt my leg — with an object) or just 다쳤어요 (I got hurt).
**많이** (manhi) = 'much / a lot / badly' — here it modifies the degree of injury: 많이 다쳤어요? = 'were you badly hurt?'
Note pronunciation: 많이 is pronounced [마니] — ㄶ cluster simplifies to ㄴ, and the ㅎ disappears before a vowel.
괜찮아요 (gwaenchanayo) is one of the most versatile Korean expressions: 'I'm okay / it's fine / no problem / don't worry'. You'll hear and use it constantly. The informal version is 괜찮아 (gwaenchana).
마세요 (maseyo) is the polite negative imperative: 'please don't do...'. 걱정 마세요 (don't worry), 늦지 마세요 (don't be late). The base is 말다 (malda) = to stop / don't. Informal: 걱정 마 (geokjeong ma).

sentences.base_language: Tomorrow will be a better day.
sentences.target_language: 내일은 더 좋은 날이 될 거예요.
sentences.romanized: Naeireun deo joeun nari doel geoyeyo.
sentences.literal: Tomorrow more good day become will.
sentences.grammarNote:
**더** (deo) = 'more' — the comparative marker placed before the adjective: 더 좋은 (better, literally 'more good').
**될 거예요** = 'will become' — 되다 (to become) + -(으)ㄹ 거예요 (future intention/prediction).
**날** (nal) = day — a native Korean word for a day in the sense of a 'day experienced.' Compare: 일 (il) = day on a calendar or a count of days.
**내일은** uses the topic marker 은 to contrast tomorrow with today — 'as for tomorrow (unlike today), it will be a better day.'
